case: 28-year-old patient, who has suffered a high-energy frontal traffic accident, has a deformity of the lower right limb in induction and internal hip rotation. It is striking that any attempt at manipulation causes him intense pain. We should think that the patient has:
first_argument: - Option 4 correct: we describe the typical mechanism of a posterior coxofemoral dislocation [axial force on the femur with the hip flexed (#high energy front traffic accident#)] that causes a typical position of the patient with posterior coxofemoral dislocation (#adduction# and #internal rotation# of the hip). - Options 1, 3 and 5 incorrect: they would produce an attitude in abduction and external rotation (@adduction@ and @internal rotation@ of the hip) - Option 2 incorrect: it would produce an attitude in abduction and external rotation (@adduction@ and @internal rotation@ of the hip)
relations_raw:
(accidente de tráfico frontal de gran energía, support, 4)
(aducción, support, 4)
(rotación interna, support, 4)
(aducción, attack, 1)
(rotación interna, attack, 1)
(aducción, attack, 3)
(rotación interna, attack, 3)
(aducción, attack, 5)
(rotación interna, attack, 5)

(aducción, attack, 2)
(rotación interna, attack, 2)

evidence_relations.evidence: accidente de tráfico frontal de gran energía

neutralized_argumentation: This case illustrates the importance of considering both the mechanism and the position of the patient when diagnosing a posterior coxofemoral dislocation. The patient's history of high-energy front traffic accident, along with their presentation in adduction and internal rotation, support this diagnosis. While options 1, 3, and 5 could be considered, the characteristic presentation of this type of dislocation should raise suspicion for this specific condition.

case: A 13-month-old infant who goes to the emergency room due to fever up to 39° C of 48 hours of evolution without another associated symptomatology. Organ and device scan without significant findings, highlighting good general condition. You were going to give him the discharge home but the pediatric attaché who is on call asks him for a urine systematic and a tube-based uroculture. In the urine, leukocyturia ++, haematuria + and nitrites ++ and in the urine Gram there are negative bacilli Gram. In the blood analysis there is no leukocytosis and the C-reactive protein is 50 mg/L. The deputy now tells him that the child does not need to enter and to be given an oral antibiotic. Point out the appropriate empirical treatment MENOS in this case:
first_argument: The most common pathogen in lower urinary tract infections (#leucocyturia#, #hematuria#, #nitrites#, #no leukocytosis#, #reactive C protein is 50 mg/L#), is Escherichia coli, even more so when Gram says that #gram negative bacillus# are observed.This micro-organism has a high resistance rate to amoxicillin alone, and less to the rest of antibiotics.
relations_raw:
(leucocituria, support, 1)
(hematuria, support, 1)
(nitritos, support, 1)
(no existe leucocitosis, support, 1)
(proteína C reactiva, support, 1)
(bacilos Gram negativos, support, 1)
neutralized_argumentation: The patient presents with symptoms suggestive of an uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection (UTI) but with abnormal urinalysis findings. The most common pathogen is Escherichia coli, even more so when Gram staining reveals #gram negative bacillus#. However, this microorganism has a high resistance rate to amoxicillin alone, and less to the rest of antibiotics. We should consider empirical ciprofloxacin therapy in light of these findings.
